coredata.dc:description: In this study, S-methyl derivatives of dithiocarbamates (DTCs) were shown to significantly enhance chemiluminescence (CL) between Ce(IV) and efficient and environmentally friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Based on the elucidation of the CL mechanisms, an innovative approach involving high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with N-CDs and CL detection (HPLC-CDCL) was proposed. The developed method was successfully applied to the highly sensitive detection of three DTC fungicides (dimethyl dithiocarbamate, ethylene bisdithiocarbamate, and propylene bisdithiocarbamate) in tea. The recovery of the established method ranged 70.51–116.45%, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of <9.40%. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) was as low as 0.19 μg/L (as CS2), which is superior to the previous methods and comparable to UPLC-tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS). Moreover, the proposed approach does not require solid-phase extraction and offers excellent selectivity. This study proposes a novel method for the detection of DTCs in the food safety and environmental fields.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, S-methyl derivatives of dithiocarbamates (DTCs) were shown to significantly enhance chemiluminescence (CL) between Ce(IV) and efficient and environmentally friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). Based on the elucidation of the CL mechanisms, an innovative approach involving high-performance liquid chromatography coupled with N-CDs and CL detection (HPLC-CDCL) was proposed. The developed method was successfully applied to the highly sensitive detection of three DTC fungicides (dimethyl dithiocarbamate, ethylene bisdithiocarbamate, and propylene bisdithiocarbamate) in tea. The recovery of the established method ranged 70.51–116.45%, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of <9.40%. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) was as low as 0.19 μg/L (as CS2), which is superior to the previous methods and comparable to UPLC-tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS). Moreover, the proposed approach does not require solid-phase extraction and offers excellent selectivity. This study proposes a novel method for the detection of DTCs in the food safety and environmental fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dithiocarbamates (DTCs) are named for their structure, where two oxygen atoms in carbamates are replaced by sulfur atoms (Campanale, Triozzi, Ragonese, Losacco, & Massarelli, 2023). They not only exhibit broad-spectrum activity against plant pathogens, particularly fungi, but also show efficacy against bacteria, plants, and insects (Guo et al., 2023; Z. Wang et al., 2020). They are characterized by cost-efficiency, short half-life, low toxicity to mammals, and low susceptibility to resistance (Mujawar, Utture, Fonseca, Matarrita, & Banerjee, 2014), making them still one of the most widely used pesticides in modern agriculture (Tian et al., 2023). For example, DTCs are widely used in the treatment of two prevalent diseases in tea crops, fusarium dieback and gray blight, in several tea-growing countries (Pandey et al., 2024; Pandey, Hubbali, Vandana, Dutta, & Babu, 2023). In addition to being used as pesticides, DTCs are commonly employed to soil treatments, fungicides in pulp and paper manufacturing, rubber production, as well as lubricants, wood processing (Crnogorac & Schwack, 2009; Schmidt, Christensen, Petersen, Sloth, & Poulsen, 2013; Veiga-del-Baño, Martínez-López, Pérez-Lucas, Cuenca-Martínez, & Andreo-Martínez, 2023). The widespread and excessive use of DTCs as fungicides has made them one of the most frequently detected agrochemicals in food samples (Jafari et al., 2012; Li et al., 2019; O. López-Fernández, Rial-Otero, González-Barreiro, & Simal-Gándara, 2012).

DTCs fungicides can be divided into three categories based on their carbon skeleton structure: dimethyldithiocarbamates (DMDTCs) such as thiram, ziram, and ferbam; ethylenebisdithiocarbamates (EBDTCs) such as mancozeb, metiram, zineb, maneb, and nabam; and propanebisdithiocarbamates (PBDTCs) such as propineb. DTCs disrupt metabolic processes by releasing ethylenebisdithiocarbamate sulfide, which chelates metal ions in enzymes or binds to thiol groups in the cytoplasm and mitochondrial enzymes (Stadler et al., 2022). There is sufficient evidence that DTCs are harmful to aquatic organisms, poultry (Liu et al., 2022), rodents (Yan Zhang et al., 2022) and fish (van Boxtel, Kamstra, Fluitsma, & Legler, 2010; Y. Wang et al., 2021), including thrombocytopenia (Huang et al., 2019), hepatorenal toxicity (J. Chen, Akhtar, & Hardej, 2023) and impaired embryonic development (Y. Wang et al., 2021). In the presence of water or oxygen, EBDTCs and PBDTCs are readily degraded into ethylenethiourea (ETU) and propylenethiourea (PTU), respectively. Unfortunately, ETU and PTU could inhibit thyroid synthesis and result in neurotoxicity, even induce carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic effects (Blasco, Font, & Picó, 2004; Bonnechère, Hanot, & Van Loco, 2011). Consequently, several DTCs have been classified as hazardous compounds by the WHO Health Organization (WHO, 2020). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop sensitive, reliable, and rapid analytical methods for detecting DTCs residues in foods.

Except for DMDTCs, the DTCs are metallic coordination compounds of metals and dithiocarbamic acid. However, they are poorly soluble in water and other organic solvents. Therefore, direct extraction and separation are not feasible. One analytical approach for DTCs is based on their decomposition into nonspecific CS2 in acidic SnCl2 solutions. The generated CS2 was assessed by coupling the system with different analytical strategies such as spectrophotometry (Caldas, Conceiçao, Miranda, de Souza, & Lima, 2001), GC (Royer, Ménand, Grimault, & Communal, 2001) and GC/MS (da Silva, Wickert, Pizzutti, & de Kok, 2021; Mujawar et al., 2014). These methods can only measure the total amount of CS2 produced irrespective of the DTCs that are present in food and environmental samples (O. López-Fernández et al., 2012). Sometimes false-positive results may occur due to endogenous CS2 in plants (O. López-Fernández et al., 2012). The alternative analytical approach includes atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS)(Cassella, Salim, Garrigues, Santelli, & de la Guardia, 2002; Soylak, Elzain Hassan Ahmed, & Ozalp, 2022), atomic emission spectroscopy (Han et al., 2018), capillary electrophoresis (Aulakh, Fekete, Malik, Mahajan, & Schmitt-Kopplin, 2007), electrochemical analysis (Olalla López-Fernández et al., 2015; Silva & De Souza, 2017), fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy (Chen et al., 2023; Cheng et al., 2022; C. Lin et al., 2023), chemiluminescence (CL)(Asghar, Yaqoob, Munawar, & Nabi, 2022a, 2022b; Paz & Catala-Icardo, 2008), and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (Fateixa, Raposo, Nogueira, & Trindade, 2018; Tsen, Yu, Chen, Lin, & Chuang, 2021). This analytical strategy is based on the detection of DTCs by the indirect detection of metals. It can only detect specific types of DTCs or the total amount of DTCs pesticides containing the same metal. Furthermore, EBDTCs fungicides are being phased out in several countries. For instance, mancozeb has been banned by the European Union since February 2021, according to its regulations (European Commission, 2020). It is expected that the requirement for the categorization of DTCs will increase in the future.

An additional approach is based on the derivatization of DTCs into compounds that are soluble in organic phases and easily separable for detection. Classical methods were first proposed by Gustafsson et al.(Gustafsson & Fahlgren, 1983; Gustafsson & Thompson, 1981). In the presence of DTC anions in basic EDTA conditions, DTC pesticides can form ion-pair products with tetraalkylammonium cations that can be extracted in organic solvents. Subsequent S-alkylation of the ion-pair compounds reacting with iodomethane results in the formation of S-methyl DTCs (DTCMe). DTCMe can be separated using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and detected by UV/DAD (Gustafsson & Fahlgren, 1983; Gustafsson & Thompson, 1981) or mass spectrometry (MS)(Sayed, Hussein, & Omran, 2022; Tsen et al., 2021). However, UV/DAD detection suffers from matrix interference and has limited sensitivity. MS presents additional challenges. The high concentration of tetraalkylammonium ion-pairing reagents (m/z 242) significantly overlapped with m/z (241) of the EBDTCs S-dimethyl derivative, hindering accurate qualitative and quantitative analyses. To prevent ion source contamination by the ion-pairing reagents, further purification via solid-phase extraction (SPE) is necessary before measurement. This undoubtedly leads to high costs and is time-consuming.

Chemiluminescence (CL) offers a widely used analytical approach (Cai et al., 2023; Li et al., 2019; Teng, Qi, Liu, Li, & Lu, 2023; L. Zhao et al., 2023) based on light emission during redox reactions. This technique boasts several advantages, including the absence of an external light source, low instrument cost, ease of operation, wide linear range, and low detection limits (El-Maghrabey, Suzuki, Kishikawa, & Kuroda, 2021; Kishikawa, El-Maghrabey, Kawamoto, Ohyama, & Kuroda, 2023). Nakazawa et al. employed ion-pair chromatography and a post-column K3Fe(CN)6-luminol CL system to detect two DTCs compounds (PBDTCs and EBDTCs). However, poor reproducibility of chromatography and weak interference resistance hinder its practical application (Nakazawa et al., 2004). Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of nanomaterials that have attracted significant interest owing to their diverse synthetic routes, affordability, environmental friendliness, and biocompatibility. They have received widespread attention in various fields such as chemistry (Li et al., 2018), biology (Othman et al., 2024), food safety (Ding et al., 2023), the environment (Y. Zhang et al., 2021) and medicine (Z. Lin et al., 2024a). However, the potential of DTC analysis remains unclear.

Interestingly, DTCs, followed by alkaline EDTA extraction and S-methylation of S-methyl derivatives, significantly enhanced the CL of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs). Subsequently, the variables influencing chromatographic separation coupled with CL detection based on N-CDs (HPLC-CDCL) were studied. The corresponding CL mechanisms were discussed in detail. Finally, the proposed HPLC-CDCL method was applied to various tea samples to detect the DTCs.

L-cysteine (L-Cys), iodomethane (CH3I), tetrabutylammonium hydrogen sulfate (TBAHS), disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2EDTA), citric acid (CA), l-serine (L-Ser), cerium sulfate tetrahydrate (Ce2SO4·4H2O), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), and methanesulfonic acid (MSA) are analytical grade reagents and from Aladdin Biochemical Scientific Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ziram (97%) was purchased from Macklin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China), and zineb (95%) and propineb (98%) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Scientific Co., Ltd. Chromatography-grade methanol, chloroform, and hexane were obtained from Merck (Germany). Water purified with a Direct-Q5 UV ultrapure water system (≥18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore) was used for all though the experiments.

To prepare an alkaline Na2EDTA solution, 37.24 g of Na2EDTA and 2.5 g of) L-Cys were dissolved in approximately 300 mL of deionized water. The pH of solution was adjusted to 10.00 ± 0.50 with 5.0 M sodium hydroxide solution. A final volume of 500 mL was obtained by dilution with ultrapure water. TBAHS (13.60 g) was dissolved in water (100 mL) to give a 0.40 M TBAHS solution. As for the Ce(IV) solution, 4.04 g of cerium(IV) sulphate was dissolved in 100 mL of 5.0 M H2SO4 boiling solution. After cooling, water was added to reach a final volume of 100 mL, resulting in a 0.10 M Ce(IV) solution in 5.0 M H2SO4.

The morphology of the N-CDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) using a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The crystalline structures of the N-CD samples were analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a D8-Advance X-ray diffractometer. The elemental composition and functional groups of the N-CDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an ESCA Lab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). A Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to characterize the functional groups of the N-CDs and their precursors. The FL and CL properties of the N-CDs were measured using an F-280 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin Gangdong Scientific Co., Ltd., China). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Finally, electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were obtained using an EPR200M electron spin resonance spectrometer (CIQTEK, China).

The N-CDs were synthesized using a one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis method with anhydrous CA and L-Ser as precursors. Briefly, equal amounts (0.50 g each) of anhydrous CA and L-Ser were mixed in a 30 mL PTFE crucible. Ultrapure water (2.5 mL) was added to dissolve the mixture. The crucible was placed in a microwave oven and pyrolyzed at 600 W for 8 min. After cooling, a dark-brown gel-like solid was obtained. This solid was redissolved in ultrapure water and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to remove any insoluble impurities, resulting in a dark brown solution. The solution was further purified via dialysis (MWCO 100 Da) for 12 h to remove free impurities with low molecular weights. The final volume was adjusted to 50 mL and the solution was stored at 4 °C and protected from light until further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and preparation of standard solutions
ce:section.ce:para: Individual standard stock solutions of zineb, thiram, and propineb were prepared at a concentration of 200 μg/mL by dissolving appropriate amounts in an alkaline Na2EDTA solution. These stock solutions were stored at −18 °C. A series of mixed standard solutions with concentrations ranging 1–800 μg/L were prepared by diluting the stock solutions with alkaline Na2EDTA solution. S-methylation was performed according to established procedures (Gustafsson & Fahlgren, 1983). The prepared solutions were diluted to the desired concentrations using the mobile phase. Standard curves were constructed by the HPLC-CDCL method.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic diagram of the HPLC-CDCL system used for the detection of DTCs. The LC separation section comprises a Waters 515 pump (Milford, MA, USA), a Rheodyne 7725i manual injection valve (Cotati, CA, USA), a Poroshell 120 EC-C8 column (150 × 4.6 mm, 4 μm) (Agilent, Waldbronn, Germany), and a column oven (Tianjin Chenhang Technology Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China). The post-column CDCL detector consists of a peristaltic pump (BT100-1F, Baoding Longer Precision Pump Co. Ltd., China) for delivering aqueous N-CDs solution and acidic Ce(IV) solution at 3.0 mL/min. The N-CD solution was mixed with the column effluent and combined with an acidic Ce(IV) solution in a homemade quartz flow cell (2 mm i.d.) to induce rapid CL. PTFE tubes (0.5 mm i. d.) were used to connect all components within the flow system.

Tea samples were obtained from the local markets in Nanchang, China. The pretreatment procedures involved slight modifications based on the methods described in SN/T 0711–2011 of the People's Republic of China and in previous literatures (Gustafsson & Fahlgren, 1983). Briefly, tea samples were oven-dried at 40 °C to constant weight were ground by mortar and screened through a 20-mesh sieve, and then further dried again at 4 °C under light protection. Accurately weighed 2.000 g of the tea powder was transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-capped centrifuge tube. Then, 30.0 mL of alkaline Na2EDTA solution was added, followed by vortexing for 2 min, and ultrasonic extraction for 20 min. Subsequently, TBAHS solution (5.0 mL) was added, and the solution was adjusted to approximately pH 7.5 using 2 M hydrochloric acid. To initiate the S-methylation reaction, 150 μL of iodomethane was added, and the mixture underwent ultrasound-assisted reaction at 20 °C for 30 min. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, the supernatant was collected in a separate funnel. Extraction of the remaining residue was repeated thrice with 30 mL of a 3:1 (v/v) chloroform-cyclohexane mixture. The combined organic phases were then dehydrated with anhydrous sodium sulfate and concentrated by rotary evaporation at 50 °C. The residue was redissolved in 2 mL of 45% methanol solution, filtered through a 0.22 μm filter and analyzed using the HPLC-CDCL method.

The DTC content of the samples was expressed as CS2 using the following eq. (1):
(1)
X i
=
A i
×
C s
× V
A s
× m
× F
where X
i
: the content of DTCs in the sample (expressed as CS2, μg‧kg−1)；A
i : peak area of the DTCs in the sample solution; C
s : concentration of the DTCs standard solution (μg‧mL−1); V: final volume of the sample solution after adjustment (mL); A
s : peak area of the DTCs in the standard solution; m: mass of the final sample solution (g); and F: conversion factor of DTCs expressed as CS2 (0.4983 for ziram, 0.5512 for zineb, and 0.5245 for propineb).

To evaluate the performance of the proposed HPLC-CDCL method in detecting the three DTCs fungicides in tea, a previously reported UPLC-MS/MS method (Sayed et al., 2022) was used, with slight modifications. A Waters ACQUITY UPLC system coupled to an AB SCIEX API 4000+ MS system was used for separation and detection of the three DTCMe. Analytes were separated on a Phenomenex Kinetex C18 column (50 × 2.1 mm, 1.3 μm) with a 10 μL sample per injection. Isocratic elution was achieved using a mobile phase composed of solvent A (methanol/water, 1:9, containing 10 mM ammonium formate, pH 4) and solvent B (pure methanol) at a ratio of 50:50 (v/v), at a flow rate of 0.3 mL/min. The total run time was 6 min. Electrospray ionization (ESI) source parameters, including the nebulizing gas, curtain gas, and collision gas, were optimized. The temperature of ion source was set to 400 °C, and the spray voltage was set to 5500 V. Table S1 details the ions used for quantitative detection of the three DTCs pesticides, along with their corresponding retention times, optimal declustering potential (DP), collision energy (CE), collision cell exit potential (CXP), and entrance potential (EP).

All experiments were carried out in three replicates, and results were displayed as mean ± SD. Data were analyzed using a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), followed by Duncan's test. p < 0.05 was considered significant.

Fig. 2c shows the XPS survey spectrum of the N-CDs, revealing typical peaks of the three elements with binding energies of 284.6, 399.6 and 531.0 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The atomic composition of the N-CDs was C1s (64.96%), N1s (9.26%), and O1s (25.78%) (Figs. 2d∼2f). In Fig. 2d, the deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectrum reveals five peaks located at 284.0, 284.6, 286.0, 287.1 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of CC, CC, CN/CO, CN/CO, and OCO in the central carbon. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2e) indicated the presence of two deconvoluted peaks with binding energies of 398.6 and 400.3 eV, attributed to sp
3 CN/CNC (pyrrole-N) and sp
2 CN (pyridine-N), respectively, suggesting N successfully doping in N-CDs. The high-resolution spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2f) could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 531.3 eV (CO), 532.4 eV (ester bond OCO), and 533.7 eV (carboxylic acid OCO(H)), representing the binding energies of oxygen in different functional groups. In summary, N-CDs possess excellent water solubility owing to the presence of a large number of hydrophilic functional groups, such as carboxyl, amide, and hydroxyl groups.

The N-CD solution exhibited a bright blue colour under 365 nm UV light (inset of Fig. 3a). To characterize the optical properties of the prepared N-CD aqueous solution, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy were employed. The UV–Vis spectrum in Fig. 3a showed two broad absorption bands at 240 and 380 nm, which were assigned to π → π* transitions in the aromatic CC sp
2 domain and n → π* transitions in CO and CN bonds, respectively. These are the characteristic absorption peaks commonly observed in CDs solution (Sajwan, Lakshmi, & Solanki, 2021). The FL spectrum of the N-CDs in Fig. 3b exhibits a noticeable redshift in the maximum emission peak as the excitation wavelength increases from 300 to 420 nm. This indicates the dependence of the emission on the excitation wavelength, demonstrating the tunable emission properties of these CDs (Kar, Praveenkumar, Si, Panigrahi, & Mishra, 2024). In addition, the maximum FL emission and excitation wavelengths were 460 and 380 nm, respectively, with good symmetry.

Four types of chromatographic columns, including Diamonsil Plus C18-A (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm), Xbridge C18 (150 × 4.6 mm, 3.5 μm), Xselect@HSS T3 (150 × 4.6 mm, 3.5 μm), and Poroshell 120 EC-C8 (150 × 4.6 mm, 4 μm), were evaluated for the separation of the three DTCMe. The results indicated that all these columns achieved complete separation for the three studied DTCMe, whereas the EC-C8 column exhibited better performance. Therefore, it was selected for further analyses.

For successful setup of the HPLC-CDCL method, a suitable mobile phase should match the requirements for efficient separation of the analytes and compatibility with the post-column CL system. Although it has been reported in the literatures (Gustafsson & Fahlgren, 1983; Gustafsson & Thompson, 1981; Jafari et al., 2012) that two common organic solvents, methanol and acetonitrile, can be used as mobile phases for the separation of the three DTCMe. Acetonitrile reacts directly with Ce(IV) and strongly inhibits CL signals, rendering it incompatible as a mobile phase for the present CDCL system. Therefore, methanol was chosen as the organic solvent for HPLC separation. Since the three DTCMe possess enough polarity in EC-C8 column, an isocratic separation condition was selected with 45% methanol as mobile phase, flow rate of 1.2 mL‧min−1, and column temperature of 35 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of synthesis conditions of N-CDs on CL
ce:section.ce:para: Many parameters, including the precursor ratio, microwave power, and synthesis time, can change the properties of the synthesized N-CDs and have a significant impact on the CL signals. To investigate the influence of the precursor ratio on CL signals, N-CDs were prepared with different mass ratios. The total mass of the precursor was fixed at 1.0 g, and the ratio of CA to L-Ser was varied. The microwave power (600 W) and synthesis time (8 min) were maintained constant. Standard solutions containing 100, 200, and 200 μg/L DTCMe of ziram, zineb, and propineb were selected to assess the performance of the method. As shown in Fig. S2a, the N-CDs prepared at a 1:1 ratio exhibited the strongest CL emissions for all three DTCMe. Thus, a 1:1 mass ratio PL and UV–Vis used for the N-CD synthesis in this study.

Because the microwave power directly influences the reaction temperature during N-CD synthesis, N-CDs with equal mass ratios of precursors were manufactured at three different power levels. As shown in Fig. S2b, the CL signals of the three DTCMe reached a maximum at a power of 600 W. When the power was <600 W, the carbonation of the precursors was insufficient, and the yields of N-CDs were inadequate, whereas an excessively high power resulted in the loss of functional groups.
The effect of the synthesis time on the CL was examined (Fig. S2c). The maximum intensity of CL signals for the three DTCMe was observed at a reaction time of 8 min.
In summary, N-CDs with optimal performance were synthesized under the conditions of 600 W, synthesis time of 8 min, and an equivalent mass ratio of the two precursors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of other parameters on CDCL
ce:section.ce:para: For highly sensitive detection of the three DTCMe, other parameters related to the DTCMe-N-CDs-Ce(IV) CL system were optimized using a univariate approach, such as the content of N-CDs, type of acidic medium and its concentration, concentration of Ce(IV), and flow rate of the peristaltic pump.

The concentration of the N-CDs played a crucial role as luminescent reagents in the CDCL system. Fig. S3a shows that, although the CL signal of DMMe decreased proportionally with the decreasing N-CD concentration, DMMe generated the strongest CL intensity among the studied DTCMe. In contrast, the CL signals of EBMe and PBMe peaked at a dilution ratio of 2500. Consequently, a dilution ratio of 2500 was selected for subsequent experiments.

As an oxidant, Ce (IV) was critical for the proposed CL system. The concentration of Ce(IV) in the range 0.2–1.2 mM was investigated (Fig. 3b). The CL intensity of all three derivatives was strongest at a concentration of 0.6 mM. Next, the CL intensity remains almost constant. So, 0.6 mM was selected as the optimal Ce (IV) concentration for the subsequent experiments.

The effects of various acidic media, such as sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, trifluoroacetic acid, methanesulfonic acid, and acetic acid, on CL were investigated. As shown in Fig. S3c, all the studied acids, except acetic acid, exhibited no significant difference in light emission for EBMe and PBMe at equivalent concentrations of hydrogen ions. However, a significantly higher CL intensity was generated in the hydrochloric acid medium for DMMe compared to the other media, making it the optimal choice. Furthermore, the influence of the concentration of hydrochloric acid in the Ce(IV) solution (0–0.12 M) on the CL signals was examined (Fig. S3d). DMMe, EBMe, and PBMe exhibited different performances. When the concentration of hydrochloric acid was 0.02 M, the CL signals of three studied DTCMe was equivalent. After that, DMMe and EBMe reached its maximum CL intensity at 0.06 M and 0.04 M, respectively, whereas the CL intensity of PBMe decreased gradually. Finally, a Ce(IV) solution containing 0.04 M hydrochloric acid was selected for further experiments.

The flow rate of CL reagents is vital for the collection of light emissions. As illustrated in Fig. S3e, when the flow rate of the peristaltic pump was <3.0 mL/min, the CL intensity was almost linear with the flow rate. Therefore, a flow rate of 3.0 mL/min was selected for subsequent experiments.

This method was validated according to the 2002/657/EC Decision (European Commission, 2002). Under optimized experimental conditions, standard chromatograms of various concentrations of DTCMe were obtained using the HPLC-CDCL method (Fig. 4a). The retention times for DMME, EBMe, and PBMe were 5.05, 8.56, and 11.63 min, respectively, with a successful baseline separation within 13 min. The analytical merits of the proposed method in terms of the calibration curve, linear range, and limit of detection (LOD) were summarized in Table S2. The CL intensity exhibited a strong linear relationship (r ≥ 0.998) with the concentration of three DTCMe at ppb levels (Fig. S4 and Table S2). The LOD (S/N = 3) was as low as 0.19 μg/L (expressed as CS2).

In Table 1 , the proposed HPLC-CDCL method is listed along with previously reported analytical techniques. The LOD of the present method is superior to that of the techniques reported in the literature and is similar to that of UPLC-MS/MS methods. Furthermore, the developed method does not require solid-phase extraction, which is essential for UPLC-MS/MS methods to eliminate the ion-pair reagents used in pretreatment procedures. Additionally, the HPLC-CDCL approach eliminates the high cost of MS equipment and high energy consumption, leading to significantly lower carbon emissions and cost-effective alternatives.

The intra- and interday recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSD) of the tea samples were measured according to the procedure described in section 2.5. Additionally, the results obtained using the HPLC-CDCL method were compared with those obtained using UPLC-MS/MS, as shown in Table 2 . The intraday RSD ranged 1.91–9.12%, whereas the interday RSD ranged 2.65–9.40%. In addition, the intra-day recoveries ranged 73.21–116.45%, and the inter-day recoveries ranged 70.51–115.59%.

Chromatographic separation of the standard and spiked samples analyzed using the proposed HPLC-CDCL method is shown in Fig. 4a and Fig. S5. DTCMe was effectively separated on an EC-C8 column using methanol and water (45:55%) as the mobile phases. Furthermore, although the tea matrix is typically complex, there was no interference in the quantification of the three DTC derivatives because of the high selectivity of the present HPLC-CDCL method. As shown in Table 2, DMDTC residues were detected in all tea samples, whereas no EBDTCs or PBDTCs residues were detected. Moreover, the data from the newly established method were comparable to those obtained using UPLC-MS/MS.

To elucidate the CL mechanisms of the Ce(IV)-N-CDs-DTCMe system, CL spectra were monitored to identify the emitter (Fig. 5 ). The maximum emission wavelength of DTCMe was consistently located at 484 nm (Fig. 5b∼d), suggesting a shared CL mechanism.

The variation between the central CL emission (484 nm) and FL of the N-CDs (455 nm) suggests that the N-CDs undergo chemical transformations during the CL process. Therefore, the FL spectrum of the solution containing the Ce(IV)-treated N-CDs was recorded. The results revealed the presence of Ce(III) and a new FL peak at 484 nm, which was attributed to oxidized N-CDs (Ox-N-CDs). Similarly, the FL spectrum of the Ce(IV)-N-CDs-DTCMe mixture exhibited a maximum emission at 484 nm. These findings strongly suggest that the excited state of Ox-N-CDs (Ox-N-CDs⁎) acts as a light emitter in this system.

One hypothesis is related to the presence of sulfur atoms in DTCMe. It is possible that Ce(IV) reacts with these compounds to generate excited sulfur dioxide (SO2 ⁎). This excited intermediate can transfer energy to the fluorescent molecules via CL resonance energy transfer (CRET), leading to light emission. The experiments were designed to detect SO2 formation by monitoring the volatile gas in the mixed solution during the reaction; however, negative results were obtained. Additionally, when quinine, a well-known CRET acceptor of SO2 ⁎, was substituted with N-CDs in the presence of Ce(IV)- DTCMe, no CL was observed. Hence, SO2 is not produced in the CL reaction.

In previous studies, reactive oxygen species (ROS) have been shown to play a role in CD-related CL mechanisms. Common radical scavengers, such as ascorbic acid, nitrotetrazolium blue chloride, sodium azide, thiourea, and p-benzoquinone, readily react with acidic Ce(IV), quenching the CL signal and rendering them unsuitable for investigating ROS in this system. Furthermore, the CL signals remained constant when the dissolved oxygen in the solution was disturbed by bubbling oxygen or nitrogen. Additionally, using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine and dimethylpyrrolidine oxide as ROS capture probes, no signals were detected by ESR spectroscopy. Therefore, ROS did not play a role in CL in this system.

In contrast, the CL spectra of Ce(IV) and N-CDs were located at 484 nm with a slightly lower intensity than that of the Ce(IV)-N-CDs-DTCMe CL system. This observation supports the mechanism outlined in Scheme 1 . Here, Ce(IV) directly oxidizes N-CDs, and the energy released during this redox reaction is transferred to the Ox-N-CDs during the formation of its excited state, Ox-N-CDs⁎. As the Ox-N-CDs⁎ return to their ground state, they emit light at 484 nm. When DTCMe is introduced, it acts as a catalyst in the aforementioned redox reaction and promotes the generation of stronger CL signals.

This study presents a novel approach for the simultaneous detection of three different types of DTCs using the HPLC-CDCL technique based on the catalytic effect of DTCMe on the CL reaction between Ce(IV) and N-CDs. Several advantages are present, including a stable baseline, low background, and high sensitivity comparable to those of UPLC-MS/MS. Furthermore, the developed method affords more effective pretreatment procedures than UPLC-MS/MS because of its excellent selectivity for sulfur-containing carbamates and high resistance to ion-pair reagents. The proposed method was successfully applied to different types of tea samples. This study opens new avenues for exploring the theoretical applications of carbon nanomaterials and the practice of HPLC coupled with CL detection.
